attributes.stem:
A 12-hours-old infant is being reviewed as the nurse has noticed the baby has been experiencing respiratory distress and cyanosis. This has only developed in the last hour. He was born at 37 weeks with a normal vaginal delivery. It is noted that the mother had missed multiple antenatal screening appointments.

On examination the neonate has increased work of breathing with intercostal recessions and a respiratory rate of 70/min. He is peripherally cyanosed and has a scaphoid appearance to his abdomen with decreased breath sounds on the left side of the chest.

A chest X-ray that includes the upper part of abdomen is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This infant has the clinical signs and imaging appearance that is consistent with a congenital diaphragmatic hernia. A scaphoid shaped abdomen is typical for diaphragmatic hernia. Furthermore, the X-ray shows bowel filling the chest, mediastinal shift and reduced bowel shadows in the abdomen. Lung markings are not seen in the left hemithorax. The large single gas shadow in the left side of the upper abdomen probably represents the swallowed air in the stomach.

In congenital diaphragmatic hernia, the defect in the diaphragm in more than 80% of cases is situated in the posterolateral aspect of the left hemidiaphragm and is known as the Bochdalek type.

Hiatus hernias are rarely seen in a neonate. Furthermore, they often show a retrocardiac opacity with a gas fluid level on X-ray, and less likely to cause such early and severe respiratory distress.

In most cases, cystic lung disease is caused by congenital pulmonary airway malformations, which form multicystic masses of lung tissue. The cystic changes can be confined to one lobe or may involve more than one lobe. The babies can be symptomatic from birth or present later, depending on the degree of lung involvement. The distribution of bowel gas in the abdomen in these cases is normal.

Neonatal ascites presents with a distended, enlarged abdomen. Lung markings will be seen in both hemithoraces. Neonatal ascites is unlikely to cause respiratory distress or cyanosis by itself, unless there is a component of a more serious condition such as hydrops fetalis.

Situs inversus is when the arrangement of the internal organs of a patient are mirrored from normal anatomy. This does not cause severe infant respiratory distress unless there are associated serious cardiac anomalies. Also, bowel shadows will not be seen in the thoracic cavity in imaging.
learningPoints: Respiratory distress in a neonate in the presence of scaphoid abdomen  is  a characteristic  feature of  congenital diaphragmatic hernia.
Congenital diaphragmatic hernia commonly results from a defect in the posterolateral part of the left diaphragm. This leads to protrusion of the abdominal contents into the left thoracic cavity.

In suspected congenital diaphragmatic hernia,  the chest radiograph taken should include the chest and the major part of the abdomen.  This would confirm the sparsity of the bowel shadows in the abdomen. Also, the continuity of the bowel  shadow extending from the abdomen into the chest can be seen.
references: Snoek KG, Reiss IK, Greenough A, Capolupo I, Urlesberger B, Wessel L, Storme L, Deprest J, Schaible T, van Heijst A, Tibboel D; CDH EURO Consortium. Standardized Postnatal Management of Infants with Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia in Europe: The CDH EURO Consortium Consensus - 2015 Update. Neonatology. 2016;110(1):66-74.

Spellar K, Gupta N. Diaphragmatic Hernia. [Updated 2020 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2021 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK536952/

Dumpa V, Chandrasekharan P. Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia. [Updated 2020 Dec 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2021 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK556076/
